0: Analysis of beneficial management practices to mitigate environmental impacts in dairy production systems around the Great Lakes

Daesoo Kim , Nick Stoddart S, C. Alan Rotz b, Karin Veltman, Larry Chase , Joyce Cooper, Pete Ingraham ¹, R. César Izaurralde8 Curtis D. Jones Richard Gaillard ¹, Horacio A. Aguirre-Villegas Rebecca A. Larson, Matt Ruark William Salas, Olivier Jollietc, Gregory J. Thoma?

b Pasture Systems and Watershed Management Research Unit, USDA-ARS, University Park, PA 16802, USA

ARTICLE INFO|ABSTRACT Keywords: Life cycle assessment Greenhouse gas emission Environmental impact Beneficial management practice Dairy production system|The influence of farm-specific beneficial management practices (BMPs) on a set of comprehensive environmental impacts was characterized and quantified for two representative dairy farms in the Great Lakes region (a large 1500-cow farm in New York (NY) and a smaller 150-cow farm in Wisconsin (WI)). Comparative benefits or drawbacks of the effect of the adoption of selected management scenarios to environmental impacts were es- timated by coupling the output from the Integrated Farm System Model (IFSM) to provide lifecycle inventory data for SimaPro The small dairy farm in WI generated a consistently larger carbon footprint than the large dairy farm in NY due to greater enteric methane (CH4) emissions from different feeds fed and greater nitrous oxide (N20) emissions from the bedded pack housing facility for young stock. The flare scenario of burning biogas produced in a covered manure storage on the small farm and a whole-farm mitigation plan of combined feed, field, and manure management in the large farm demonstrate significant potential to reduce overall carbon footprint by 20.0% and 25.8%, respectively, compared to the baselines. The assessments of selective impact categories such as fossil energy use, water use, land occupation, aquatic eutrophication, terrestrial acidification, respiratory effects, human toxicity, and ecotoxicity are discussed and highlight hot spots relevant for sustainable dairy farm management. Normalization analysis indicates that eutrophication potential is the largest relative impact profile, which suggests that efforts to mitigate eutrophication can achieve relatively greater environ- mental impact reduction. Although this study identifies the beneficial adaptation of sustainable dairy production practices on individual impact profiles, trade-offs between impact categories make the analysis more complex when considering the comprehensive suite of environmental impacts.

2: Agriculture, one of the most extensive human activities on the planet, is responsible for potential greenhouse gas emissions (GHGEs) as well as adverse environmental impacts on ecosystems (McLaughlin

and Mineau, 1995). Substantial endeavors are underway nationally and globally to assess and alleviate the risks that agro-ecosystems have on the natural environment. Integrating mitigation and adaptation strate- gies is recognized to be distinctly vital in any comprehensive approach to managing the environmental impacts of agricultural production

systems (Ayers and Huq, 2009; Wilbanks et al., 2003). In consort with many industrial sectors, the livestock sector, particularly the dairy in- dustry, has also been actively involved in relevant research to establish sustainable and efficient practices. This project targeted research in the area of climate change adaptation and mitigation efforts in dairy pro- duction systems around the Great Lakes region of the United States (USDA, 2017), and this paper presents a broad-view life cycle impact assessment of the potential benefit of management practices for redu- cing environmental impacts.

Milk consumption is well-known to have positive effects on human health (Weaver, 2010). According to the U.S. Dietary Guidelines for Americans, three cups or servings of dairy products are recommended per day for most calorie levels (USDHHS and USDA, 2015). However, dairy production systems, especially at farm level, are known to be a contributor to potential environmental issues including climate change, aquatic and terrestrial eutrophication, and acidification (Asgedom and Kebreab, 2011; Ogino et al., 2008; Thomassen and de Boer, 2005; Thomassen et al., 2008; Veltman et al., 2017; Vergé et al., 2007). In 2016, the U.S. had 9.3 million dairy cows and produced 96.4 million metric tons of fluid milk from 40,220 licensed farms (USDA, 2018). The average herd size of U.S. dairy farms grew to 234 cows in 2017, an increase of 4.7% from 2016 (USDA, 2018). As dairy operations grow larger, concerns about environmental issues have been raised, and the prospects of future intensification to meet increased demand for food, including dairy products may have further detrimental effects on ecosystems (Tilman, 1999). Therefore, the U.S. dairy industry including farmers has committed to environmental leader- ship in promoting efforts to produce dairy products in sustainably efficient ways. Several mitigation technologies and practices, commonly known as beneficial management practices (BMPs), in both crop and animal pro- duction systems, have been recommended to meet increased economic and environmental sustainability goals (Smith et al., 2008). Even though there are studies showing that some of the management practices may increase costs (MacLeod et al., 2010; Pellerin et al., 2017; Schneider et al., 2007), in general, BMPs are acknowledged to promote land stewardship, increase net economic return and reduce environmental impacts (Asgedom and Kebreab, 2011). However, there is still a need for further evaluation of combinations of management practices to improve whole- farm sustainability (Yan et al., 2013).

Feed production, enteric fermentation, manure management, and farm operations are identified to be the major contributors to the environmental impacts of dairy farms (Castanheira et al., 2010; Fantin et al., 2012; Gerber et al., 2010; Gerber et al., 2013; Thoma et al., 2013b). The Integrated Farm System Model (IFSM), which is a tool for whole farm environmental analysis, simulates all major farm components on a process level by in- tegrating the many biological and physical processes in a farm production system (Rotz et al., 2018). The IFSM has been effectively used to predict GHGEs from dairy and beef production systems (Rotz et al., 2010; Rotz et al., 2019; Sejina et al., 2011; Stackhouse-Lawson et al., 2012; Veltman et al., 2018). However, IFSM does not include a comprehensive suite of environmental impact categories for the systems studied. Thus, we used the IFSM simulation to generate and integrate life cycle inventory data of all major farm components into an LCA computational modeling platform, SimaPro 8.5 (PRé Consultant Inc., 2018). SimaPro computes lifecycle impact assessments including many other impact categories as listed in Table 1. Our objective was to evaluate the effectiveness of multiple BMP scenarios across a comprehensive suite of environmental impact categories for two distinct dairy farm types in different locations using a cradle-to- gate life cycle assessment (LCA) approach. This study helps identify en- vironmental impact reduction opportunities for improving sustainability and supporting profitable milk production systems across the North-cen- tral and North-eastern U.S.

5: Following the recent work of Veltman et al. (2018), BMP scenarios were evaluated for two dairy farming regions around the Great Lakes

with a smaller herd of 150 cows (WI) and a larger herd of 1500 cows (NY). Representative baseline scenarios were identified based on cur- rent farm management practices for comparison. The baseline scenario and selected BMP scenarios were defined in terms of herd character- istics (number of lactating cows, dry cows, young and older heifers, and replacement animals), feed rations (total feed, animal group specific feed storage and rations), field management (crop area, type, and yield, planting and harvest dates, associated field management practices, and crop rotations), manure management (storage type, length of storage, size of storage tank, existence of cover, processing: use of anaerobic digester and/or solid-liquid separator), manure application (amount, date, application method), synthetic fertilizer application by crop (amount, date, application method), and barn characteristics (barn type, barn size, ventilation system, manure management including manure removal frequency).
6: <header>2.1. Goal and scope, functional unit and system boundary </header>

The principal goal of the study was to increase understanding of the significant variables in dairy production systems driving environmental impact profiles (Table 1) and to determine key indicators for sustain- able performance. SimaPro 8.5 (PRé Consultant Inc., 2018) with the DATASMART database (Long Trail Sustainability, 2016) was used as the primary modeling software to measure and analyze the environ- mental impacts and to identify the hotspots within the system boundary of the study. The functional unit was 1 kg of fat and protein corrected milk (FPCM, 4% fat and 3.3% protein) at the farm gate which has been chosen to account for variations in milk characteristics (IDF, 2015). The system boundaries are from cradle to farm-gate inclusive of on- and off- farm crop production through milk extraction. To distribute the input and output flows of a system and to assign environmental impacts be- tween co-products of milk and beef, we chose a biophysical allocation approach following Thoma et al. (2013a). A mass-based allocation was used for sold crops and co-products exported from the farm to remain consistent with IFSM calculations (IDF, 2015).

We adapted the IMPACT World midpoint assessment methodology, which is the update of the IMPACT 2002 LUCAS, and EDIP methods (Bulle et al., 2019). The IMPACT World + is a globally regionalized life cycle impact assessment (LCIA) method and provides characterization factors to assess the multiple environmental impact indicators chosen for this study. We modified global warming characterization factors (for in- stance, methane biogenic: 27.8kg CO2-eq, methane fossil: 30.5kg CO2-eq and nitrous oxide: 265 kg CO2-eq) adopted from the most recent IPCC GWP 100a emission factors (IPCC, 2014). IFSM, which simulates crop production, feed use and nutrient cycling over 25 years of local weather, adapts the IPCC tier 3 methodological approach in estimating emissions associated with farm activities (Rotz et al., 2018). IFSM also adapts a tier 2 approach for non-climate-dependent specific activities such as methane emissions from accumulated manure in a bedded pack and storage in stacks (Rotz et al., 2018).

We used the AWARE method for water footprint (Boulay et al., 2018) because the water use indicator in IMPACT World + was not the most up to date. Characterization factors of some substances that are not defined in the IMPACT World + method were identified and mapped from other available methods such as IPCC and ReCiPe. Human toxicity (carcinogenic and non-carcinogenic) and ecotoxicity impacts were based on the parameterized version of USEtox developed by an international team from the United Nations Environment Program (UNEP) and the Society for Environmental Toxicology and Chemistry (SETAC) to provide midpoint characterization factors for human tox- icological and ecotoxicological impacts of chemical emissions (Rosenbaum et al., 2008). USEtox evaluates impacts through the si- mulation of the release of chemicals into the environment. Character- ization factors for heavy metals are only provided for metals released in dissolved form. In our analysis, human toxicity was represented by combining the impact categories of carcinogens or non-carcinogens for
Table 1 Environmental impact profiles adopted from IMPACT World + midpoint assessment method.

Impact category|Unit|Characterization model reference Global warming|kg CO2 eq.|IPCC 2013 100a (IPCC, 2014) Fossil energy use|MJ deprived|Cumulative energy demand (Hischier et al., 2010) Water use|m³ deprived|AWARE (Details in Section 3.2; Boulay et al., 2018) Land occupation|ha.yr arable|Eco-indicator 99 (Goedkoop and Spriensma, 2000) Aquatic eutrophication|kg PO4 eq.|Global hydrological dataset, CML (Guinee et al., 2002) Marine eutrophication|kg Neq.|EDIP 2003 (Hischier et al., 2010) Terrestrial acidification|kg SO2 eq.|Eco-indicator 99 (Goedkoop and Spriensma, 2000) Aquatic ecotoxicity|CTUe|IMPACT 2002 (Jolliet et al., 2003) Respiratory inorganics|kg PM2.5 eq.|IMPACT 2002 (Jolliet et al., 2003) Respiratory organics|kg NMVOC eq.|IMPACT 2002 (Jolliet et al., 2003) Human toxicity|CTUh|IMPACT 2002 (Jolliet et al., 2003)
short-term and long-term, indoor emissions, and pesticide residues.

9: <header>2.1. Goal and scope, functional unit and system boundary </header>

The principal goal of the study was to increase understanding of the significant variables in dairy production systems driving environmental impact profiles (Table 1) and to determine key indicators for sustain- able performance. SimaPro 8.5 (PRé Consultant Inc., 2018) with the DATASMART database (Long Trail Sustainability, 2016) was used as the primary modeling software to measure and analyze the environ- mental impacts and to identify the hotspots within the system boundary of the study. The functional unit was 1 kg of fat and protein corrected milk (FPCM, 4% fat and 3.3% protein) at the farm gate which has been chosen to account for variations in milk characteristics (IDF, 2015). The system boundaries are from cradle to farm-gate inclusive of on- and off- farm crop production through milk extraction. To distribute the input and output flows of a system and to assign environmental impacts be- tween co-products of milk and beef, we chose a biophysical allocation approach following Thoma et al. (2013a). A mass-based allocation was used for sold crops and co-products exported from the farm to remain consistent with IFSM calculations (IDF, 2015).

We adapted the IMPACT World midpoint assessment methodology, which is the update of the IMPACT 2002 LUCAS, and EDIP methods (Bulle et al., 2019). The IMPACT World + is a globally regionalized life cycle impact assessment (LCIA) method and provides characterization factors to assess the multiple environmental impact indicators chosen for this study. We modified global warming characterization factors (for in- stance, methane biogenic: 27.8kg CO2-eq, methane fossil: 30.5kg CO2-eq and nitrous oxide: 265 kg CO2-eq) adopted from the most recent IPCC GWP 100a emission factors (IPCC, 2014). IFSM, which simulates crop production, feed use and nutrient cycling over 25 years of local weather, adapts the IPCC tier 3 methodological approach in estimating emissions associated with farm activities (Rotz et al., 2018). IFSM also adapts a tier 2 approach for non-climate-dependent specific activities such as methane emissions from accumulated manure in a bedded pack and storage in stacks (Rotz et al., 2018).

We used the AWARE method for water footprint (Boulay et al., 2018) because the water use indicator in IMPACT World + was not the most up to date. Characterization factors of some substances that are not defined in the IMPACT World + method were identified and mapped from other available methods such as IPCC and ReCiPe. Human toxicity (carcinogenic and non-carcinogenic) and ecotoxicity impacts were based on the parameterized version of USEtox developed by an international team from the United Nations Environment Program (UNEP) and the Society for Environmental Toxicology and Chemistry (SETAC) to provide midpoint characterization factors for human tox- icological and ecotoxicological impacts of chemical emissions (Rosenbaum et al., 2008). USEtox evaluates impacts through the si- mulation of the release of chemicals into the environment. Character- ization factors for heavy metals are only provided for metals released in dissolved form. In our analysis, human toxicity was represented by combining the impact categories of carcinogens or non-carcinogens for

10: Table 2 describes the characteristics of the two baseline dairy farms, commonly found in each region, with a description of four principal farm components. These farm activities were identified and developed based on consultation with external experts (Dane County Conserva- tionists, Madison, WI) for the small farm (150 Holstein milking cows including dry cows with 130 replacement heifers; 40% replacement rate) and a previously-studied commercial dairy farm for the large farm (1500 Holstein milking cows including dry cows with 1180 replace- ment heifers; 38% replacement rate) (Veltman et al., 2018). Most of the male calves and a small part of the female calves not needed for herd replacement were sold shortly after birth at the average weight of 45 kg to the beef industry. Raw milk production was 27.8 kg FPCM/cow/day for the small farm and 26.1 kg FPCM/cow/day for the large farm. Milk production varied slightly depending on the BMP as shown in Fig. S1 in Supplementary Material. IFSM used cost-minimizing linear program- ming to formulate rations for six herd groups (young heifer, older heifer, dry cow, early lactating cow, mid lactating cow, and late lac- tating cow) to meet their nutrient requirements (Rotz et al., 2018). The rations fed to herds including purchased feed were composed of corn silage, alfalfa silage, oat silage, low-quality grass hay, high moisture corn grain, dry grain, soybean meal, a less degradable protein supple- ment, and mineral mixtures (Fig. S2).
The forage content in the rations was set to average 65% of total dry matter intake (DMI) across lactating cow groups and feed conversion

efficiency was about 1.5 kg FPCM per kg of feed DMI for both farms. Crops including alfalfa, corn, oats, and grass were cultivated on 130 ha of cropland on the small farm and 12801 of cropland on the large farm. Milking COWS were housed in free stall barns with natural venti- lation in the small farm, and a mechanical ventilation system was used in the large farm with straw bedding. One difference to note is that the replacement heifers on the small farm were housed in a bedded-pack barn. Manure handling was also slightly different between farms. Manure was handled as a slurry (8-10% DM) except for the bedded- pack manure, which was a solid form applied in the spring and fall on the small farm. Both farms applied manure on the field by broadcast application without incorporation.

12: Identification and potential adoption of BMPs were determined around each baseline dairy farm's activities in consultation with spe- cialists of each farm component. Table 3 presents the details of selected BMPs with different characteristics depending on farm size (Hristov et al., 2013; Knapp et al., 2014; Montes et al., 2013). Three specific farm components, known to influence the majority of environmental impacts, feed, field and manure management, coupled with combined feed and integrated whole-farm mitigation plans were evaluated in the analysis.

The feed and manure management BMPs were selected based on their expected or known potential to reduce direct and indirect GHG emissions. These BMPs were supplemented with five field interventions expected or known to reduce nutrient losses. The primary objective of

feed management practices was higher milk production and lower GHGEs. Increased corn silage or reduced forage content in rations tar- gets to reduce enteric methane emissions by improving the digestibility of the diet or by inhibiting the growth of methane-producing micro- organisms (Knapp et al., 2014). Increased digestibility of neutral de- tergent fiber (NDF) from 60% to 62% (Hristov et al., 2013) and in- creased feed efficiency from 1.5 to 1.65kg milk per kg feed DMI (Vandehaar et al., 2016) target increased milk production per cow. Increased fat content in the diet targets increased milk production as well as decreased enteric methane production (Knapp et al., 2014). These BMP adjustments are within the range of known or recommended variations and are achievable relative to the baseline, which is the current feed characteristics of a representative farm. Reduced protein in the diet of lactating cows through more precise feed supplementation reduces nitrogen excretions and related emissions (Montes et al., 2013). Combinations of these feed BMPs (Feed 1, 2 and 3) were a final strategy used in an attempt to achieve the goal.

Field management BMPs cover a variety of issues including cover crops, no-till practices, and manure application practices (Duncan et al., 2017; Hou et al., 2015; Krueger et al., 2012; Rotz, 2004; Shipitalo et al., 2013; Syswerda et al., 2012). These approaches target increased crop yields with more efficient nutrient utilization and reduced nitrous oxide and ammonia emissions. Manure management BMPs primarily targeted reduced methane and nitrous oxide emissions (Holly et al., 2017; Hou et al., 2015; Montes et al., 2013; Rotz, 2004). The decomposition of

manure under anaerobic conditions produces methane, while the ni- trification-denitrification processes release nitrous oxide. Manure BMPs included the use of solid-liquid separation and anaerobic digestion on the large farm or a covered storage with flaring of methane produced during storage on the small farm.

Integrated whole-farm mitigation plans were developed by selecting the best performing individual BMPs. Figs. S3 and S4 present the BMP inventory from IFSM simulations of both farms for feed intake and as- sociated enteric methane emissions, respectively. Enteric methane production in IFSM is related to fiber and starch contents of the diet as well as dry matter and energy intake. Tables S1 through S6 present IFSM simulation output data including crop harvest area, crop yield and use, nutrient flows, and annual emissions of important gaseous com- pounds from farm activities, which were used as input data to SimaPro for both farms. In each of the scenarios, the herd size was held constant, purchases of crops and protein mixtures were minimized, and the same amount of manure was exported to ensure a consistent assessment and comparison among the baseline and BMP results.

simulation of all major farm components. This enables integration of IFSM's outputs into SimaPro as an LCA tool to provide detailed life cycle inventory data. In IFSM, the simulation begins with crop production for corn, small grains, soybeans, alfalfa, and pasture. During this portion of the simulation, we captured inventory information including resource use and specific nutrient application by crop. IFSM has been designed to report information, primarily at whole-farm scale. To include a more granular view, we modified the source code to extract information before ag- gregation to the whole-farm scale; thus, information for individual crops and animal classes was extracted and linked to corresponding activities in the lifecycle model in SimaPro We captured inventory, by crop, for soil nutrient dynamics including the amount of nitrogen (N) and phosphorus (P) leaching and runoff. Once crops are produced, they are stored until formulated into feed rations, and we collected storage related volatile organic compound (VOC) emissions. The model continues with ration determination and feeding of the six herd groups. Here we collected the necessary herd statistics and ration consumption. Manure generated by the herd is applied in crop production. These data were captured on a monthly time step with statistics evaluated at the end of the simulation. We used existing data structures to produce and, in some cases, back solve for re- quired inventory values including diesel fuel and electricity consumption for farm activities, ammonia and methane emissions generated by manure storage and housing, and some field level emissions.

Solid digestate is used for bedding, and liquid digestate is used for land application Combined feed|Feed 1|Large, small|Combination of feed management BMPs: High CS, Low Forage, High Dig, High Eff, High Fat, and Low Protein Combined feed|Feed 2|Large, small|High CS, High Dig, High Eff, High Fat, and Low Protein Combined feed|Feed 3|Large, small|Same as Feed 2 scenario with rye silage double-cropped after corn silage Whole-farm mitigation plans|Mitigation 1|Large|Increase NDF digestibility of feeds by 2%; Increase feed conversion efficiency; reduce diet protein of lactating COWS to NRC minimum nutrient requirements; an AD is used; manure solids are separated and used for bedding Whole-farm mitigation plans|Mitigation 2|Large|Increase NDF digestibility of feeds by 2%; increase feed conversion efficiency; reduce diet protein of lactating COWS to NRC minimum nutrient requirements; an AD is used; manure solids are separated and used for bedding; a no-till system with subsurface injection of manure Whole-farm mitigation plans|Mitigation 3|Large|Low forage rations (about 50% of DMI) fed to lactating cows; Increase NDF digestibility of feeds by 2%; Increase feed conversion efficiency; reduce diet protein of lactating COWS to NRC minimum nutrient requirements; an AD is used; manure solids are separated and used for bedding; no-till system with subsurface injection of manure

feed management practices was higher milk production and lower GHGEs. Increased corn silage or reduced forage content in rations tar- gets to reduce enteric methane emissions by improving the digestibility of the diet or by inhibiting the growth of methane-producing micro- organisms (Knapp et al., 2014). Increased digestibility of neutral de- tergent fiber (NDF) from 60% to 62% (Hristov et al., 2013) and in- creased feed efficiency from 1.5 to 1.65kg milk per kg feed DMI (Vandehaar et al., 2016) target increased milk production per cow. Increased fat content in the diet targets increased milk production as well as decreased enteric methane production (Knapp et al., 2014). These BMP adjustments are within the range of known or recommended variations and are achievable relative to the baseline, which is the current feed characteristics of a representative farm. Reduced protein in the diet of lactating cows through more precise feed supplementation reduces nitrogen excretions and related emissions (Montes et al., 2013). Combinations of these feed BMPs (Feed 1, 2 and 3) were a final strategy used in an attempt to achieve the goal.

Field management BMPs cover a variety of issues including cover crops, no-till practices, and manure application practices (Duncan et al., 2017; Hou et al., 2015; Krueger et al., 2012; Rotz, 2004; Shipitalo et al., 2013; Syswerda et al., 2012). These approaches target increased crop yields with more efficient nutrient utilization and reduced nitrous oxide and ammonia emissions. Manure management BMPs primarily targeted reduced methane and nitrous oxide emissions (Holly et al., 2017; Hou et al., 2015; Montes et al., 2013; Rotz, 2004). The decomposition of

manure under anaerobic conditions produces methane, while the ni- trification-denitrification processes release nitrous oxide. Manure BMPs included the use of solid-liquid separation and anaerobic digestion on the large farm or a covered storage with flaring of methane produced during storage on the small farm.

Integrated whole-farm mitigation plans were developed by selecting the best performing individual BMPs. Figs. S3 and S4 present the BMP inventory from IFSM simulations of both farms for feed intake and as- sociated enteric methane emissions, respectively. Enteric methane production in IFSM is related to fiber and starch contents of the diet as well as dry matter and energy intake. Tables S1 through S6 present IFSM simulation output data including crop harvest area, crop yield and use, nutrient flows, and annual emissions of important gaseous com- pounds from farm activities, which were used as input data to SimaPro for both farms. In each of the scenarios, the herd size was held constant, purchases of crops and protein mixtures were minimized, and the same amount of manure was exported to ensure a consistent assessment and comparison among the baseline and BMP results.

IFSM was used to simulate alternate BMP scenarios. We program- matically extracted life cycle inventory and statistical data from IFSM to be used in SimaPro Fig. 1 is a schematic of the data linking structure be- tween IFSM and SimaPro IFSM provides a process level full system

simulation of all major farm components. This enables integration of IFSM's outputs into SimaPro as an LCA tool to provide detailed life cycle inventory data. In IFSM, the simulation begins with crop production for corn, small grains, soybeans, alfalfa, and pasture. During this portion of the simulation, we captured inventory information including resource use and specific nutrient application by crop. IFSM has been designed to report information, primarily at whole-farm scale. To include a more granular view, we modified the source code to extract information before ag- gregation to the whole-farm scale; thus, information for individual crops and animal classes was extracted and linked to corresponding activities in the lifecycle model in SimaPro We captured inventory, by crop, for soil nutrient dynamics including the amount of nitrogen (N) and phosphorus (P) leaching and runoff. Once crops are produced, they are stored until formulated into feed rations, and we collected storage related volatile organic compound (VOC) emissions. The model continues with ration determination and feeding of the six herd groups. Here we collected the necessary herd statistics and ration consumption. Manure generated by the herd is applied in crop production. These data were captured on a monthly time step with statistics evaluated at the end of the simulation. We used existing data structures to produce and, in some cases, back solve for re- quired inventory values including diesel fuel and electricity consumption for farm activities, ammonia and methane emissions generated by manure storage and housing, and some field level emissions.
15: Identification and potential adoption of BMPs were determined around each baseline dairy farm's activities in consultation with spe- cialists of each farm component. Table 3 presents the details of selected BMPs with different characteristics depending on farm size (Hristov et al., 2013; Knapp et al., 2014; Montes et al., 2013). Three specific farm components, known to influence the majority of environmental impacts, feed, field and manure management, coupled with combined feed and integrated whole-farm mitigation plans were evaluated in the analysis.

To include a more granular view, we modified the source code to extract information before ag- gregation to the whole-farm scale; thus, information for individual crops and animal classes was extracted and linked to corresponding activities in the lifecycle model in SimaPro We captured inventory, by crop, for soil nutrient dynamics including the amount of nitrogen (N) and phosphorus (P) leaching and runoff. Once crops are produced, they are stored until formulated into feed rations, and we collected storage related volatile organic compound (VOC) emissions. The model continues with ration determination and feeding of the six herd groups. Here we collected the necessary herd statistics and ration consumption. Manure generated by the herd is applied in crop production. These data were captured on a monthly time step with statistics evaluated at the end of the simulation. We used existing data structures to produce and, in some cases, back solve for re- quired inventory values including diesel fuel and electricity consumption for farm activities, ammonia and methane emissions generated by manure storage and housing, and some field level emissions.
17: Identification and potential adoption of BMPs were determined around each baseline dairy farm's activities in consultation with spe- cialists of each farm component. Table 3 presents the details of selected BMPs with different characteristics depending on farm size (Hristov et al., 2013; Knapp et al., 2014; Montes et al., 2013). Three specific farm components, known to influence the majority of environmental impacts, feed, field and manure management, coupled with combined feed and integrated whole-farm mitigation plans were evaluated in the analysis.

The feed and manure management BMPs were selected based on their expected or known potential to reduce direct and indirect GHG emissions. These BMPs were supplemented with five field interventions expected or known to reduce nutrient losses. The primary objective of

|Scenario|Farm size|Description Feed management|High corn silage|Large, small|Increase corn silage (CS) to alfalfa/grass ratio from 1:1 to 3:1 in animal diets Feed management|Low forage|Large, small|Low forage rations reduced from 65% to 50% of dry matter intake (DMI) fed to lactating cows with cropland adjusted to provide feed needed with increased corn grain and undegradable crude protein supplements Feed management|High dig|Large, small|High NDF digestibility: NDF digestibility of feeds is increased by 2% from the baseline Feed management|High eff|Large, small|Increase feed conversion efficiency from 1.5 to 1.65kg milk/kg feed DMI Feed management|High fat|Large, small|Increase supplemental fat in the diet of lactating cows from 0.4 to 0.9kg/day/cow Feed management|Low protein|Large, small|Reduce protein content of lactating cows' ration from 17% to about 14% Field management|Cover crop|Large, Small|Annual grass cover crop following corn Field management|No-till|Large, small|No-till establishment used for all crops with no incorporation of manure Field management|No-till injection|Large, small|No-till establishment used for all crops with manure applied through subsurface injection; no nitrogen (N) fertilizer used Field management|Same day|Large, small|Manure is incorporated into the soil the same day of application with N fertilizer use reduced to 40 kg N/ ha Field management|Summer applied|Large, small|9-month manure storage is emptied in the spring and again in early summer with N fertilizer use reduced to 60 kg N/ha Manure management|Digestion|Large|Anaerobic digester (AD) is used on-farm to produce electricity. The digestate is applied to cropland Manure management|Flare|Large, small|Covered manure storage is used with a flare to burn the biogas Manure management|Covered Tank|Small|The manure storage has an unsealed cover. Manure management|Separation|Large|Solid-liquid separation is conducted. Solid manure is used for bedding, and liquid manure is stored in an earthen basin and surface applied to cropland Manure management|Digestion and separation|Large|AD and post-solid-liquid separation are applied. Solid digestate is used for bedding, and liquid digestate is used for land application Combined feed|Feed 1|Large, small|Combination of feed management BMPs: High CS, Low Forage, High Dig, High Eff, High Fat, and Low Protein Combined feed|Feed 2|Large, small|High CS, High Dig, High Eff, High Fat, and Low Protein Combined feed|Feed 3|Large, small|Same as Feed 2 scenario with rye silage double-cropped after corn silage Whole-farm mitigation plans|Mitigation 1|Large|Increase NDF digestibility of feeds by 2%; Increase feed conversion efficiency; reduce diet protein of lactating COWS to NRC minimum nutrient requirements; an AD is used; manure solids are separated and used for bedding Whole-farm mitigation plans|Mitigation 2|Large|Increase NDF digestibility of feeds by 2%; increase feed conversion efficiency; reduce diet protein of lactating COWS to NRC minimum nutrient requirements; an AD is used; manure solids are separated and used for bedding; a no-till system with subsurface injection of manure Whole-farm mitigation plans|Mitigation 3|Large|Low forage rations (about 50% of DMI) fed to lactating cows; Increase NDF digestibility of feeds by 2%; Increase feed conversion efficiency; reduce diet protein of lactating COWS to NRC minimum nutrient requirements; an AD is used; manure solids are separated and used for bedding; no-till system with subsurface injection of manure
Note CS: corn silage; DMI: dry matter intake; NDF: neutral detergent fiber; AD: anaerobic digester; NRC: National Research Council.

feed management practices was higher milk production and lower GHGEs. Increased corn silage or reduced forage content in rations tar- gets to reduce enteric methane emissions by improving the digestibility of the diet or by inhibiting the growth of methane-producing micro- organisms (Knapp et al., 2014). Increased digestibility of neutral de- tergent fiber (NDF) from 60% to 62% (Hristov et al., 2013) and in- creased feed efficiency from 1.5 to 1.65kg milk per kg feed DMI (Vandehaar et al., 2016) target increased milk production per cow. Increased fat content in the diet targets increased milk production as well as decreased enteric methane production (Knapp et al., 2014). These BMP adjustments are within the range of known or recommended variations and are achievable relative to the baseline, which is the current feed characteristics of a representative farm. Reduced protein in the diet of lactating cows through more precise feed supplementation reduces nitrogen excretions and related emissions (Montes et al., 2013). Combinations of these feed BMPs (Feed 1, 2 and 3) were a final strategy used in an attempt to achieve the goal.

Field management BMPs cover a variety of issues including cover crops, no-till practices, and manure application practices (Duncan et al., 2017; Hou et al., 2015; Krueger et al., 2012; Rotz, 2004; Shipitalo et al., 2013; Syswerda et al., 2012). These approaches target increased crop yields with more efficient nutrient utilization and reduced nitrous oxide and ammonia emissions. Manure management BMPs primarily targeted reduced methane and nitrous oxide emissions (Holly et al., 2017; Hou et al., 2015; Montes et al., 2013; Rotz, 2004). The decomposition of

manure under anaerobic conditions produces methane, while the ni- trification-denitrification processes release nitrous oxide. Manure BMPs included the use of solid-liquid separation and anaerobic digestion on the large farm or a covered storage with flaring of methane produced during storage on the small farm.

Integrated whole-farm mitigation plans were developed by selecting the best performing individual BMPs. Figs. S3 and S4 present the BMP inventory from IFSM simulations of both farms for feed intake and as- sociated enteric methane emissions, respectively. Enteric methane production in IFSM is related to fiber and starch contents of the diet as well as dry matter and energy intake. Tables S1 through S6 present IFSM simulation output data including crop harvest area, crop yield and use, nutrient flows, and annual emissions of important gaseous com- pounds from farm activities, which were used as input data to SimaPro for both farms. In each of the scenarios, the herd size was held constant, purchases of crops and protein mixtures were minimized, and the same amount of manure was exported to ensure a consistent assessment and comparison among the baseline and BMP results.

IFSM was used to simulate alternate BMP scenarios. We program- matically extracted life cycle inventory and statistical data from IFSM to be used in SimaPro Fig. 1 is a schematic of the data linking structure be- tween IFSM and SimaPro IFSM provides a process level full system

simulation of all major farm components. This enables integration of IFSM's outputs into SimaPro as an LCA tool to provide detailed life cycle inventory data. In IFSM, the simulation begins with crop production for corn, small grains, soybeans, alfalfa, and pasture. During this portion of the simulation, we captured inventory information including resource use and specific nutrient application by crop. IFSM has been designed to report information, primarily at whole-farm scale. To include a more granular view, we modified the source code to extract information before ag- gregation to the whole-farm scale; thus, information for individual crops and animal classes was extracted and linked to corresponding activities in the lifecycle model in SimaPro We captured inventory, by crop, for soil nutrient dynamics including the amount of nitrogen (N) and phosphorus (P) leaching and runoff. Once crops are produced, they are stored until formulated into feed rations, and we collected storage related volatile organic compound (VOC) emissions. The model continues with ration determination and feeding of the six herd groups. Here we collected the necessary herd statistics and ration consumption. Manure generated by the herd is applied in crop production. These data were captured on a monthly time step with statistics evaluated at the end of the simulation. We used existing data structures to produce and, in some cases, back solve for re- quired inventory values including diesel fuel and electricity consumption for farm activities, ammonia and methane emissions generated by manure storage and housing, and some field level emissions.
20: Identification and potential adoption of BMPs were determined around each baseline dairy farm's activities in consultation with spe- cialists of each farm component. Table 3 presents the details of selected BMPs with different characteristics depending on farm size (Hristov et al., 2013; Knapp et al., 2014; Montes et al., 2013). Three specific farm components, known to influence the majority of environmental impacts, feed, field and manure management, coupled with combined feed and integrated whole-farm mitigation plans were evaluated in the analysis.

The feed and manure management BMPs were selected based on their expected or known potential to reduce direct and indirect GHG emissions. These BMPs were supplemented with five field interventions expected or known to reduce nutrient losses. The primary objective of

|Scenario|Farm size|Description Feed management|High corn silage|Large, small|Increase corn silage (CS) to alfalfa/grass ratio from 1:1 to 3:1 in animal diets Feed management|Low forage|Large, small|Low forage rations reduced from 65% to 50% of dry matter intake (DMI) fed to lactating cows with cropland adjusted to provide feed needed with increased corn grain and undegradable crude protein supplements Feed management|High dig|Large, small|High NDF digestibility: NDF digestibility of feeds is increased by 2% from the baseline Feed management|High eff|Large, small|Increase feed conversion efficiency from 1.5 to 1.65kg milk/kg feed DMI Feed management|High fat|Large, small|Increase supplemental fat in the diet of lactating cows from 0.4 to 0.9kg/day/cow Feed management|Low protein|Large, small|Reduce protein content of lactating cows' ration from 17% to about 14% Field management|Cover crop|Large, Small|Annual grass cover crop following corn Field management|No-till|Large, small|No-till establishment used for all crops with no incorporation of manure Field management|No-till injection|Large, small|No-till establishment used for all crops with manure applied through subsurface injection; no nitrogen (N) fertilizer used Field management|Same day|Large, small|Manure is incorporated into the soil the same day of application with N fertilizer use reduced to 40 kg N/ ha Field management|Summer applied|Large, small|9-month manure storage is emptied in the spring and again in early summer with N fertilizer use reduced to 60 kg N/ha Manure management|Digestion|Large|Anaerobic digester (AD) is used on-farm to produce electricity. The digestate is applied to cropland Manure management|Flare|Large, small|Covered manure storage is used with a flare to burn the biogas Manure management|Covered Tank|Small|The manure storage has an unsealed cover. Manure management|Separation|Large|Solid-liquid separation is conducted. Solid manure is used for bedding, and liquid manure is stored in an earthen basin and surface applied to cropland Manure management|Digestion and separation|Large|AD and post-solid-liquid separation are applied. Solid digestate is used for bedding, and liquid digestate is used for land application Combined feed|Feed 1|Large, small|Combination of feed management BMPs: High CS, Low Forage, High Dig, High Eff, High Fat, and Low Protein Combined feed|Feed 2|Large, small|High CS, High Dig, High Eff, High Fat, and Low Protein Combined feed|Feed 3|Large, small|Same as Feed 2 scenario with rye silage double-cropped after corn silage Whole-farm mitigation plans|Mitigation 1|Large|Increase NDF digestibility of feeds by 2%; Increase feed conversion efficiency; reduce diet protein of lactating COWS to NRC minimum nutrient requirements; an AD is used; manure solids are separated and used for bedding Whole-farm mitigation plans|Mitigation 2|Large|Increase NDF digestibility of feeds by 2%; increase feed conversion efficiency; reduce diet protein of lactating COWS to NRC minimum nutrient requirements; an AD is used; manure solids are separated and used for bedding; a no-till system with subsurface injection of manure Whole-farm mitigation plans|Mitigation 3|Large|Low forage rations (about 50% of DMI) fed to lactating cows; Increase NDF digestibility of feeds by 2%; Increase feed conversion efficiency; reduce diet protein of lactating COWS to NRC minimum nutrient requirements; an AD is used; manure solids are separated and used for bedding; no-till system with subsurface injection of manure

feed management practices was higher milk production and lower GHGEs. Increased corn silage or reduced forage content in rations tar- gets to reduce enteric methane emissions by improving the digestibility of the diet or by inhibiting the growth of methane-producing micro- organisms (Knapp et al., 2014). Increased digestibility of neutral de- tergent fiber (NDF) from 60% to 62% (Hristov et al., 2013) and in- creased feed efficiency from 1.5 to 1.65kg milk per kg feed DMI (Vandehaar et al., 2016) target increased milk production per cow. Increased fat content in the diet targets increased milk production as well as decreased enteric methane production (Knapp et al., 2014). These BMP adjustments are within the range of known or recommended variations and are achievable relative to the baseline, which is the current feed characteristics of a representative farm. Reduced protein in the diet of lactating cows through more precise feed supplementation reduces nitrogen excretions and related emissions (Montes et al., 2013). Combinations of these feed BMPs (Feed 1, 2 and 3) were a final strategy used in an attempt to achieve the goal.

Field management BMPs cover a variety of issues including cover crops, no-till practices, and manure application practices (Duncan et al., 2017; Hou et al., 2015; Krueger et al., 2012; Rotz, 2004; Shipitalo et al., 2013; Syswerda et al., 2012). These approaches target increased crop yields with more efficient nutrient utilization and reduced nitrous oxide and ammonia emissions. Manure management BMPs primarily targeted reduced methane and nitrous oxide emissions (Holly et al., 2017; Hou et al., 2015; Montes et al., 2013; Rotz, 2004). The decomposition of

manure under anaerobic conditions produces methane, while the ni- trification-denitrification processes release nitrous oxide. Manure BMPs included the use of solid-liquid separation and anaerobic digestion on the large farm or a covered storage with flaring of methane produced during storage on the small farm.

Integrated whole-farm mitigation plans were developed by selecting the best performing individual BMPs. Figs. S3 and S4 present the BMP inventory from IFSM simulations of both farms for feed intake and as- sociated enteric methane emissions, respectively. Enteric methane production in IFSM is related to fiber and starch contents of the diet as well as dry matter and energy intake. Tables S1 through S6 present IFSM simulation output data including crop harvest area, crop yield and use, nutrient flows, and annual emissions of important gaseous com- pounds from farm activities, which were used as input data to SimaPro for both farms. In each of the scenarios, the herd size was held constant, purchases of crops and protein mixtures were minimized, and the same amount of manure was exported to ensure a consistent assessment and comparison among the baseline and BMP results.

Field management BMPs cover a variety of issues including cover crops, no-till practices, and manure application practices (Duncan et al., 2017; Hou et al., 2015; Krueger et al., 2012; Rotz, 2004; Shipitalo et al., 2013; Syswerda et al., 2012). These approaches target increased crop yields with more efficient nutrient utilization and reduced nitrous oxide and ammonia emissions. Manure management BMPs primarily targeted reduced methane and nitrous oxide emissions (Holly et al., 2017; Hou et al., 2015; Montes et al., 2013; Rotz, 2004). The decomposition of

manure under anaerobic conditions produces methane, while the ni- trification-denitrification processes release nitrous oxide. Manure BMPs included the use of solid-liquid separation and anaerobic digestion on the large farm or a covered storage with flaring of methane produced during storage on the small farm.

Integrated whole-farm mitigation plans were developed by selecting the best performing individual BMPs. Figs. S3 and S4 present the BMP inventory from IFSM simulations of both farms for feed intake and as- sociated enteric methane emissions, respectively. Enteric methane production in IFSM is related to fiber and starch contents of the diet as well as dry matter and energy intake. Tables S1 through S6 present IFSM simulation output data including crop harvest area, crop yield and use, nutrient flows, and annual emissions of important gaseous com- pounds from farm activities, which were used as input data to SimaPro for both farms. In each of the scenarios, the herd size was held constant, purchases of crops and protein mixtures were minimized, and the same amount of manure was exported to ensure a consistent assessment and comparison among the baseline and BMP results.

IFSM was used to simulate alternate BMP scenarios. We program- matically extracted life cycle inventory and statistical data from IFSM to be used in SimaPro Fig. 1 is a schematic of the data linking structure be- tween IFSM and SimaPro IFSM provides a process level full system

simulation of all major farm components. This enables integration of IFSM's outputs into SimaPro as an LCA tool to provide detailed life cycle inventory data. In IFSM, the simulation begins with crop production for corn, small grains, soybeans, alfalfa, and pasture. During this portion of the simulation, we captured inventory information including resource use and specific nutrient application by crop. IFSM has been designed to report information, primarily at whole-farm scale. To include a more granular view, we modified the source code to extract information before ag- gregation to the whole-farm scale; thus, information for individual crops and animal classes was extracted and linked to corresponding activities in the lifecycle model in SimaPro We captured inventory, by crop, for soil nutrient dynamics including the amount of nitrogen (N) and phosphorus (P) leaching and runoff. Once crops are produced, they are stored until formulated into feed rations, and we collected storage related volatile organic compound (VOC) emissions. The model continues with ration determination and feeding of the six herd groups. Here we collected the necessary herd statistics and ration consumption. Manure generated by the herd is applied in crop production. These data were captured on a monthly time step with statistics evaluated at the end of the simulation. We used existing data structures to produce and, in some cases, back solve for re- quired inventory values including diesel fuel and electricity consumption for farm activities, ammonia and methane emissions generated by manure storage and housing, and some field level emissions.
23: Normalization determines the relative significance of different ca- tegories in environmental impact profiles. It provides guidance to support target activities on those impacts where meaningful allevia- tions are possible. The assessment results are normalized by dividing the impact per unit of production by the total impact of the reference economy of the specific impact category per person per year. In this case, to simplify interpretation, the emissions contributing to each impact category were normalized on the basis of per capita milk pro- duction for the U.S. (297kg of milk produced per capita in 2016, (USDA, 2018)). Because the IMPACT World assessment framework did not have U.S. specific normalization factors, this analysis was conducted using the modified Stepwise 2006 method (2.-0 LCA Consultants, 2018), which aggregates all impacts to a monetary unit of EURO in 2003 value. We customized normalization factors by con- verting and inflating 2003 EURO to 2016 U.S. dollars (Table S7). To remain consistent with impact assessment, we applied the most recent IPCC GWP 100a emission factors.
24: Fig. 2 presents comparative contribution analyses of GHGEs for the two dairy farms identifying specific contributing farm activities with alternative BMPs. The lowercase baseline carbon footprint of the large dairy was 0.980 kg CO2-eq/kg FPCM, and the range with BMPs was 0.727-1.03kg CO2-eq/kg FPCM at the farm gate. For the small dairy, the baseline was 1.22kg CO2-eq/kg FPCM and BMPs fell in the range of 0.974-1.30kg CO2-eq/kg FPCM at the farm gate. These estimated carbon footprints were slightly greater than Veltman et al. (2018)'s study and were comparable to the findings of Gerber et al. (2010), Thoma et al. (2013b) and van der Werf et al. (2009). A dominant contributor and most sensitive to GHGEs of BMPs on both farms were methane emissions as shown in Figs. S5 and S6 in Supplementary Ma- terial. The small dairy farm generated uniformly greater GWP than the large dairy farm across all BMPs. Two primary contributing factors were identified: higher emissions per kg FPCM associated with ration effects on enteric methane and greater nitrous oxide emissions from the bedded-pack barn on the small farm. Considering the feed effects on enteric methane, we note that the small farm cows consumed more feed and concurrently produced more milk, but there were differences as- sociated with two feed ingredients they consumed more silage and high moisture corn grain in place of dried grain in the ration. The re- mainder of the rations between farms was quite similar. The results suggest that higher milk productivity per cow does not automatically support better environmental performance in dairy production. From the perspective of reducing GHGEs, this analysis suggests a more complex interaction among factors requiring a careful balance between milk productivity and GHGEs that include factors such as manure management.

Feed production was a notable contributor to GWP in milk pro- duction systems, contributing between 15% and 29% of total emissions across all the BMPs. Feed-related emissions at the small farm were slightly greater than at the large farm. The observed difference was a result of small differences in the rations. Considering only feed pro- duction, Feed 1 resulted in the highest emissions in percentage at both farms. This outcome occurred by increasing the corn to alfalfa silage ratio and reducing the lactating cow ration forage content from 65% to 50% of DMI while adjusting other feed ingredients in the ration as needed to meet nutritional requirements. This initial insight is more interesting when comparing enteric methane emissions from all herd

Horizontal lines indicate baseline results (lower black line for large, upper red line for small farms). groups for the baseline scenarios of both farms - 0.495 and 0.635 kg CO2-eq/kg FPCM representing 51% and 52% of total emissions, re- spectively. The composition difference between dry grain and high moisture corn grain explains the difference. As shown in both the Low Forage and Feed 1 BMPs, reducing forage content in the rations sup- presses enteric methane production, lowering overall emissions. The choice of alternative rations demonstrates the importance of interac- tions among efforts to reduce environmental impacts by correlating not only diet itself but also diet-related enteric methane emissions. The Summer Applied manure scenario shows the greatest potential to reduce GHGEs under field management practices on both farms. Other field management practices including no-till practice show slight or no GHGE reduction potential compared to the baseline. This result corresponds to Powlson et al. (2014)'s argument that the role of no-till in climate change mitigation is minimal. The introduction of anaerobic digestion for energy recovery on the large farm and combustion of methane gas generated in manure storage facilities on both farms de- monstrates substantial cumulative GWP reduction compared to the baseline. Solid-liquid separation of manure without use of a digester slightly lowered GHGEs (3.7%) compared to the baseline. The reduc- tion in emissions from the separated liquid storage was offset by emissions from the separated solids which were either used on the farm or exported (12% of collected manure solids) to off-farm uses. For the large dairy farm, Mitigation 3 among all BMPs gave the largest reduc- tion (26%) in carbon footprint relative to the baseline followed by flaring (20% reduction). For the small dairy farm, the Flare scenario presented the largest GWP reduction (16% reduction relative to the baseline) followed by Feed 1 (11% reduction). GWP was more sensitive to alternative manure management practices than feed or field man- agement practices. Scenarios based on whole-farm mitigation plans for the large farm predicted a consistent reduction in carbon footprint re- lative to the baseline.

Horizontal lines indicate baseline results (lower black line for large, upper red line for small farms). groups for the baseline scenarios of both farms - 0.495 and 0.635 kg CO2-eq/kg FPCM representing 51% and 52% of total emissions, re- spectively. The composition difference between dry grain and high moisture corn grain explains the difference. As shown in both the Low Forage and Feed 1 BMPs, reducing forage content in the rations sup- presses enteric methane production, lowering overall emissions. The choice of alternative rations demonstrates the importance of interac- tions among efforts to reduce environmental impacts by correlating not only diet itself but also diet-related enteric methane emissions. The Summer Applied manure scenario shows the greatest potential to reduce GHGEs under field management practices on both farms. Other field management practices including no-till practice show slight or no GHGE reduction potential compared to the baseline. This result corresponds to Powlson et al. (2014)'s argument that the role of no-till in climate change mitigation is minimal. The introduction of anaerobic digestion for energy recovery on the large farm and combustion of methane gas generated in manure storage facilities on both farms de- monstrates substantial cumulative GWP reduction compared to the baseline. Solid-liquid separation of manure without use of a digester slightly lowered GHGEs (3.7%) compared to the baseline. The reduc- tion in emissions from the separated liquid storage was offset by emissions from the separated solids which were either used on the farm or exported (12% of collected manure solids) to off-farm uses. For the large dairy farm, Mitigation 3 among all BMPs gave the largest reduc- tion (26%) in carbon footprint relative to the baseline followed by flaring (20% reduction). For the small dairy farm, the Flare scenario presented the largest GWP reduction (16% reduction relative to the baseline) followed by Feed 1 (11% reduction). GWP was more sensitive to alternative manure management practices than feed or field man- agement practices. Scenarios based on whole-farm mitigation plans for the large farm predicted a consistent reduction in carbon footprint re- lative to the baseline.
29: Fig. 2 presents comparative contribution analyses of GHGEs for the two dairy farms identifying specific contributing farm activities with alternative BMPs. The lowercase baseline carbon footprint of the large dairy was 0.980 kg CO2-eq/kg FPCM, and the range with BMPs was 0.727-1.03kg CO2-eq/kg FPCM at the farm gate. For the small dairy, the baseline was 1.22kg CO2-eq/kg FPCM and BMPs fell in the range of 0.974-1.30kg CO2-eq/kg FPCM at the farm gate. These estimated carbon footprints were slightly greater than Veltman et al. (2018)'s study and were comparable to the findings of Gerber et al. (2010), Thoma et al. (2013b) and van der Werf et al. (2009). A dominant contributor and most sensitive to GHGEs of BMPs on both farms were methane emissions as shown in Figs. S5 and S6 in Supplementary Ma- terial. The small dairy farm generated uniformly greater GWP than the large dairy farm across all BMPs. Two primary contributing factors were identified: higher emissions per kg FPCM associated with ration effects on enteric methane and greater nitrous oxide emissions from the bedded-pack barn on the small farm. Considering the feed effects on enteric methane, we note that the small farm cows consumed more feed and concurrently produced more milk, but there were differences as- sociated with two feed ingredients they consumed more silage and high moisture corn grain in place of dried grain in the ration. The re- mainder of the rations between farms was quite similar. The results suggest that higher milk productivity per cow does not automatically support better environmental performance in dairy production. From the perspective of reducing GHGEs, this analysis suggests a more complex interaction among factors requiring a careful balance between milk productivity and GHGEs that include factors such as manure management.

Feed production was a notable contributor to GWP in milk pro- duction systems, contributing between 15% and 29% of total emissions across all the BMPs. Feed-related emissions at the small farm were slightly greater than at the large farm. The observed difference was a result of small differences in the rations. Considering only feed pro- duction, Feed 1 resulted in the highest emissions in percentage at both farms. This outcome occurred by increasing the corn to alfalfa silage ratio and reducing the lactating cow ration forage content from 65% to 50% of DMI while adjusting other feed ingredients in the ration as needed to meet nutritional requirements. This initial insight is more interesting when comparing enteric methane emissions from all herd

31: Table 4 presents the percentage change (increase or decrease in the environmental impact of BMPs relative to the baseline scenario. This summary chart provides a clear lens into the specific activities that played a major role in the resultant impact profiles based on total emissions. Most scenarios except for cover crop, no-till and whole-farm mitigation plans on the large farm display complex trade-offs between impact profiles. For instance, in both large and small farms, decreasing forage content in the rations, which is the opposite of the current trends in the dairy industry (Chase, 2015), shows a potential to reduce carbon footprint by 7.2% and 6.1%, respectively, against the baseline scenario. In addition to lower GWP, adaptation of low forage rations in feed management practices reduces the impacts of terrestrial acidification (TA) and respiratory inorganics (RIO), but it increases other environ- mental impacts such as fossil energy use (FEU), water use (WU), aquatic eutrophication (EUT), human toxicity (HTC), and aquatic ecotoxicity (AE). These results are outcomes of two primary contributing sources: enteric methane emissions and the rate of fertilizer application to cropland, which was adjusted in the IFSM to balance with manure nutrient application as influenced by changes in animal diets. Interestingly, most BMP scenarios consistently show an increase or decrease in impacts on both farms. But not all the scenarios are direc- tionally aligned. Specifically, combined feed scenarios (Feed 1, 2 and 3) between farms display a distinct disagreement in WU and EUT caused by different rations fed to cows. In WU impact, increased undegradable crude protein supplements (distillers dried grains with solubles (DDGS), corn gluten meal, maize germ meal, and meat and bone meal) in rations from the baseline increases WU impact, and decreased degradable crude protein supplement (soybean meal) in rations reduces WU impact on both farms. But the small farm has a larger incremental ratio of undegradable crude protein supplements in rations than the large farm
Selective environmental impact change (%) associated with BMPs relative to baseline.

Note: CS: corn silage; GWP: global warming potential; FEU: fossil energy use; WU: water use; EUT: aquatic eutrophication; TA: terrestrial acidification; RIO: respiratory inorganics; HTC: human toxicity - carcinogenic; AE: aquatic ecotoxicity. Shaded area indicates larger than + 15%. Highlighted cells indicate more than 15% variations compared to the baselines.

relative to the baseline, which results in the negative impact. Similar results were observed in EUT caused by nutrient concentrations in the runoff. Another interesting case is no-till field management for which the GWP, FEU, HTC, and AE have opposite directionality - increasing from the baseline for the small farm and decreasing for the large farm.

These differences are driven by the need for increased purchasing of off-farm feeds at the small farm for this scenario. As shown at the bottom of the table, the adoption of integrated whole-farm mitigation plans leads to the greatest potential to reduce wide-ranging environ- mental risks.

Left and right columns in each BMP scenario represent impact results of large and small dairy farms, respectively. Horizontal lines indicate baseline results (black line for large, red line for small farms). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
34: Table 4 presents the percentage change (increase or decrease in the environmental impact of BMPs relative to the baseline scenario. This summary chart provides a clear lens into the specific activities that played a major role in the resultant impact profiles based on total emissions. Most scenarios except for cover crop, no-till and whole-farm mitigation plans on the large farm display complex trade-offs between impact profiles. For instance, in both large and small farms, decreasing forage content in the rations, which is the opposite of the current trends in the dairy industry (Chase, 2015), shows a potential to reduce carbon footprint by 7.2% and 6.1%, respectively, against the baseline scenario. In addition to lower GWP, adaptation of low forage rations in feed management practices reduces the impacts of terrestrial acidification (TA) and respiratory inorganics (RIO), but it increases other environ- mental impacts such as fossil energy use (FEU), water use (WU), aquatic eutrophication (EUT), human toxicity (HTC), and aquatic ecotoxicity (AE). These results are outcomes of two primary contributing sources: enteric methane emissions and the rate of fertilizer application to cropland, which was adjusted in the IFSM to balance with manure nutrient application as influenced by changes in animal diets. Interestingly, most BMP scenarios consistently show an increase or decrease in impacts on both farms. But not all the scenarios are direc- tionally aligned. Specifically, combined feed scenarios (Feed 1, 2 and 3) between farms display a distinct disagreement in WU and EUT caused by different rations fed to cows. In WU impact, increased undegradable crude protein supplements (distillers dried grains with solubles (DDGS), corn gluten meal, maize germ meal, and meat and bone meal) in rations from the baseline increases WU impact, and decreased degradable crude protein supplement (soybean meal) in rations reduces WU impact on both farms. But the small farm has a larger incremental ratio of undegradable crude protein supplements in rations than the large farm
Selective environmental impact change (%) associated with BMPs relative to baseline.

Note: CS: corn silage; GWP: global warming potential; FEU: fossil energy use; WU: water use; EUT: aquatic eutrophication; TA: terrestrial acidification; RIO: respiratory inorganics; HTC: human toxicity - carcinogenic; AE: aquatic ecotoxicity. Shaded area indicates larger than + 15%. Highlighted cells indicate more than 15% variations compared to the baselines.

relative to the baseline, which results in the negative impact. Similar results were observed in EUT caused by nutrient concentrations in the runoff. Another interesting case is no-till field management for which the GWP, FEU, HTC, and AE have opposite directionality - increasing from the baseline for the small farm and decreasing for the large farm.

These differences are driven by the need for increased purchasing of off-farm feeds at the small farm for this scenario. As shown at the bottom of the table, the adoption of integrated whole-farm mitigation plans leads to the greatest potential to reduce wide-ranging environ- mental risks.

40: Table 4 presents the percentage change (increase or decrease in the environmental impact of BMPs relative to the baseline scenario. This summary chart provides a clear lens into the specific activities that played a major role in the resultant impact profiles based on total emissions. Most scenarios except for cover crop, no-till and whole-farm mitigation plans on the large farm display complex trade-offs between impact profiles. For instance, in both large and small farms, decreasing forage content in the rations, which is the opposite of the current trends in the dairy industry (Chase, 2015), shows a potential to reduce carbon footprint by 7.2% and 6.1%, respectively, against the baseline scenario. In addition to lower GWP, adaptation of low forage rations in feed management practices reduces the impacts of terrestrial acidification (TA) and respiratory inorganics (RIO), but it increases other environ- mental impacts such as fossil energy use (FEU), water use (WU), aquatic eutrophication (EUT), human toxicity (HTC), and aquatic ecotoxicity (AE). These results are outcomes of two primary contributing sources: enteric methane emissions and the rate of fertilizer application to cropland, which was adjusted in the IFSM to balance with manure nutrient application as influenced by changes in animal diets. Interestingly, most BMP scenarios consistently show an increase or decrease in impacts on both farms. But not all the scenarios are direc- tionally aligned. Specifically, combined feed scenarios (Feed 1, 2 and 3) between farms display a distinct disagreement in WU and EUT caused by different rations fed to cows. In WU impact, increased undegradable crude protein supplements (distillers dried grains with solubles (DDGS), corn gluten meal, maize germ meal, and meat and bone meal) in rations from the baseline increases WU impact, and decreased degradable crude protein supplement (soybean meal) in rations reduces WU impact on both farms. But the small farm has a larger incremental ratio of undegradable crude protein supplements in rations than the large farm

In addition to lower GWP, adaptation of low forage rations in feed management practices reduces the impacts of terrestrial acidification (TA) and respiratory inorganics (RIO), but it increases other environ- mental impacts such as fossil energy use (FEU), water use (WU), aquatic eutrophication (EUT), human toxicity (HTC), and aquatic ecotoxicity (AE). These results are outcomes of two primary contributing sources: enteric methane emissions and the rate of fertilizer application to cropland, which was adjusted in the IFSM to balance with manure nutrient application as influenced by changes in animal diets. Interestingly, most BMP scenarios consistently show an increase or decrease in impacts on both farms. But not all the scenarios are direc- tionally aligned. Specifically, combined feed scenarios (Feed 1, 2 and 3) between farms display a distinct disagreement in WU and EUT caused by different rations fed to cows. In WU impact, increased undegradable crude protein supplements (distillers dried grains with solubles (DDGS), corn gluten meal, maize germ meal, and meat and bone meal) in rations from the baseline increases WU impact, and decreased degradable crude protein supplement (soybean meal) in rations reduces WU impact on both farms. But the small farm has a larger incremental ratio of undegradable crude protein supplements in rations than the large farm

relative to the baseline, which results in the negative impact. Similar results were observed in EUT caused by nutrient concentrations in the runoff. 48: <header>3.3. Fossil energy use, water use, and aquatic eutrophication potential </header>

As shown in Fig. 3, total fossil energy use in the large and small farms was in the range of 2.2-2.7 (similar to van der Werf et al. (2009)'s study) and 2.6-3.7 MJ deprived per kg FPCM, respectively, across all BMPs. The energy requirement for feed production is the dominant driver followed by electricity and natural gas use in the milking parlor and diesel fuel use. The magnitude of fossil energy use varies slightly among BMP scenarios. Increasing the corn:alfalfa silage ratio in the diet is the most sensitive BMP, which increases energy use on both farms. Unlike the GWP results, combined feed practices of Feed 1, 2 and 3 increase fossil energy demand relative to the baseline. Increased corn silage use with increased protein supplement fed to adjust for the re- duction in alfalfa fed is responsible for this result. Some viable choices of rations reduce GWP by lowering enteric methane production but have the potential to increase energy requirements for producing the required feeds. The results indicate that the mitigation potential in feed management practices including feed analyses and diet balancing re- quires continued evaluation.

Although water availability in the Great Lakes region has not been an issue for dairy producers, dairy farms are important water users. The assessed water use in large and small dairy farms in this study was in

the range of 87.0-163 and 80.8-1311 deprived per kg FPCM at the farm gate, respectively, depending on the BMP. Under the baseline scenario of the large farm, 1201 are deprived per kg FPCM. One thing to note is that the assessed water use impact in this analysis is defined as the amount of consumed water multiplied by a water scarcity or stress in- dicator, which is the ratio of withdrawn water to available water re- maining (Boulay et al., 2018). The water scarcity indicator is country- specific, which may not be fully representative of the specific regions studied here. For this reason, we also present water consumption (Fig. S7), which is defined as water removed physically from a watershed and not returned to the same watershed. The assessed water con- sumption for the large and small dairy farms in this study is in the range of 16.2-27.3 and 12.3-25.71 deprived per kg FPCM at the farm gate, respectively, depending on the BMP. Water consumption per kg FPCM shows that crop production contributes most of the impact followed by water use in cleaning the milking parlor. There are minor variations among BMPs except for those specific scenarios that have variation in the amount of soybean meal and undegradable protein supplements used in rations, due to irrigation water use during their production. Increasing corn:alfalfa silage ratio in the diets resulted in the largest increase among BMPs on water footprint for both the small and large dairy farms. This is a result of the need to increase soybean meal in the
ration because of the loss of alfalfa protein when the alfalfa silage quantity was reduced.

Eutrophication, which is geospatially variable and dependent on local conditions, is a widespread environmental challenge arising from excessive nutrient load and runoff, particularly nitrogen and phos- phorus, in surrounding watersheds. In this study, aquatic eutrophica- tion was mainly dominated by phosphorus (P) loss in runoff following manure and commercial fertilizer application on crop fields. Fig. 3 shows that the small farm generates uniformly greater aquatic eu- trophication potential than the large farm for all BMPs. This is primarily because of different soil characteristics. The soil of the small farm is a medium loam with 45.5% silt, 22% clay and 32.5% sand and the soil of the large farm is a shallow loam with 54% silt, 22% clay, and 24% sand. IFSM simulates soil phosphorus dynamics and losses, and because there is more P available on the small farm per unit of cropland compared to the large farm, the ultimate result is that P loss in runoff and leachate from the small farm is 0.13 g P/kg FPCM compared to the 0.054 g P/kg FPCM for the large farm. The differences in management, soils, and climate cause emission differences among farms. If all management characteristics, especially soil characteristics are maintained the same or proportional to farm size, then the P loss is similar between farms when it is expressed per animal unit.

Another interesting aspect of the small farm is that decreasing al- falfa and grass use by increasing corn silage and high moisture grain in rations dramatically increases the aquatic eutrophication potential by 29% under Feed 1 scenario compared to the baseline. Specific field management by including a winter cover crop shows potential to re- duce aquatic eutrophication impact by 7.7% and 5.4% respectively in the large and small dairy farms. Overall, reduced dietary protein achieved through more optimal inclusion of supplemental protein feeds shows the greatest potential to alleviate aquatic eutrophication poten- tial.
