There are minor variations among BMPs except for those specific scenarios that have variation in the amount of soybean meal and undegradable protein supplements used in rations, due to irrigation water use during their production. Increasing corn:alfalfa silage ratio in the diets resulted in the largest increase among BMPs on water footprint for both the small and large dairy farms. This is a result of the need to increase soybean meal in the
ration because of the loss of alfalfa protein when the alfalfa silage quantity was reduced.

Eutrophication, which is geospatially variable and dependent on local conditions, is a widespread environmental challenge arising from excessive nutrient load and runoff, particularly nitrogen and phos- phorus, in surrounding watersheds. In this study, aquatic eutrophica- tion was mainly dominated by phosphorus (P) loss in runoff following manure and commercial fertilizer application on crop fields. Fig. 3 shows that the small farm generates uniformly greater aquatic eu- trophication potential than the large farm for all BMPs. This is primarily because of different soil characteristics. The soil of the small farm is a medium loam with 45.5% silt, 22% clay and 32.5% sand and the soil of the large farm is a shallow loam with 54% silt, 22% clay, and 24% sand. IFSM simulates soil phosphorus dynamics and losses, and because there is more P available on the small farm per unit of cropland compared to the large farm, the ultimate result is that P loss in runoff and leachate from the small farm is 0.13 g P/kg FPCM compared to the 0.054 g P/kg FPCM for the large farm. The differences in management, soils, and climate cause emission differences among farms. If all management characteristics, especially soil characteristics are maintained the same or proportional to farm size, then the P loss is similar between farms when it is expressed per animal unit.

Another interesting aspect of the small farm is that decreasing al- falfa and grass use by increasing corn silage and high moisture grain in rations dramatically increases the aquatic eutrophication potential by 29% under Feed 1 scenario compared to the baseline. Specific field management by including a winter cover crop shows potential to re- duce aquatic eutrophication impact by 7.7% and 5.4% respectively in the large and small dairy farms. Overall, reduced dietary protein achieved through more optimal inclusion of supplemental protein feeds shows the greatest potential to alleviate aquatic eutrophication poten- tial.
52: <header>3.3. Fossil energy use, water use, and aquatic eutrophication potential </header>

As shown in Fig. 3, total fossil energy use in the large and small farms was in the range of 2.2-2.7 (similar to van der Werf et al. (2009)'s study) and 2.6-3.7 MJ deprived per kg FPCM, respectively, across all BMPs. The energy requirement for feed production is the dominant driver followed by electricity and natural gas use in the milking parlor and diesel fuel use. The magnitude of fossil energy use varies slightly among BMP scenarios. Increasing the corn:alfalfa silage ratio in the diet is the most sensitive BMP, which increases energy use on both farms. Unlike the GWP results, combined feed practices of Feed 1, 2 and 3 increase fossil energy demand relative to the baseline. Increased corn silage use with increased protein supplement fed to adjust for the re- duction in alfalfa fed is responsible for this result. Some viable choices of rations reduce GWP by lowering enteric methane production but have the potential to increase energy requirements for producing the required feeds. The results indicate that the mitigation potential in feed management practices including feed analyses and diet balancing re- quires continued evaluation.

Although water availability in the Great Lakes region has not been an issue for dairy producers, dairy farms are important water users. The assessed water use in large and small dairy farms in this study was in

the range of 87.0-163 and 80.8-1311 deprived per kg FPCM at the farm gate, respectively, depending on the BMP. Under the baseline scenario of the large farm, 1201 are deprived per kg FPCM. One thing to note is that the assessed water use impact in this analysis is defined as the amount of consumed water multiplied by a water scarcity or stress in- dicator, which is the ratio of withdrawn water to available water re- maining (Boulay et al., 2018). The water scarcity indicator is country- specific, which may not be fully representative of the specific regions studied here. For this reason, we also present water consumption (Fig. S7), which is defined as water removed physically from a watershed and not returned to the same watershed. The assessed water con- sumption for the large and small dairy farms in this study is in the range of 16.2-27.3 and 12.3-25.71 deprived per kg FPCM at the farm gate, respectively, depending on the BMP. Water consumption per kg FPCM shows that crop production contributes most of the impact followed by water use in cleaning the milking parlor. There are minor variations among BMPs except for those specific scenarios that have variation in the amount of soybean meal and undegradable protein supplements used in rations, due to irrigation water use during their production. Increasing corn:alfalfa silage ratio in the diets resulted in the largest increase among BMPs on water footprint for both the small and large dairy farms. This is a result of the need to increase soybean meal in the
ration because of the loss of alfalfa protein when the alfalfa silage quantity was reduced.

54: <header>3.4. Land occupation, terrestrial acidification, human toxicity, and ecotoxicity </header>

The assessment method of land occupation depends on the amount of land used and the duration of impacts on ecosystem quality from land occupation and transformation. In this study, the land occupation impact is relatively straightforward primarily dominated by the area used for crop production (Fig. 4). Two BMP scenarios of increased feed conversion efficiency and reduced protein content in diets present substantial potential on both farms to reduce land occupation impact relative to the baseline. Applying a double crop practice with rye silage after corn silage uses less land as shown in Feed 3 on both farms. Other important factors affecting land use impact are feed management practices of some BMP scenarios such as high digestibility, high feed conversion efficiency, and reduced diet protein.

Terrestrial acidification potential is estimated by evaluating the atmospheric source-deposition relationship, primarily driven by am- monia emissions. Emissions from manure application and fertilizer use in crop production are responsible for most of the impacts on both dairy farms, which are in line with other studies (Djekic et al., 2014; IDF, 2009). Ammonia emission from slurry manure applied to the crop field is the major contributor. Hydrogen sulfide emissions from the decom- posing manure on the barn floor and in manure storage followed by NOx emissions, primarily due to diesel combustion during farm activ- ities are also contributors. Among BMPs, no-till establishment used for all crops with manure applied through subsurface injection shows the highest potential to reduce the impact by 58% and 46% on the large and small dairy farms, respectively, relative to the baselines. The main reason for this result is that no inorganic N fertilizer is needed with the no-till injection scenario. On the large farm, reducing diet protein and manure separation, as well as practices for field management, demon- strate high potential to reduce terrestrial acidification. Surprisingly, field management practices such as cover crop, no-till, and summer

applied manure on the small dairy farm did not influence the impact relative to the baseline. The main causes are emissions related to crop production which involves large amounts of N fertilizer application.

Human toxicity and aquatic ecotoxicity impacts present similar trends among BMPs (Fig. 4, carcinogenic effects in human toxicity are shown in Fig. S8). Aquatic ecotoxicity presents combined short-term and long-term aquatic ecotoxicological impacts. Our findings point to crop production as a major contributor to potential human toxicity and aquatic ecotoxicity for both dairy farms. Primarily, soil and water af- fected by manure, fertilizer and pesticide applications trigger the in- creased impacts. Milking activity is the second largest contributor be- cause of the emissions caused by electricity generation. Among BMPs, two scenarios, increasing corn silage in diets and decreasing forage content in rations, are leading contributors to increasing human toxicity and aquatic ecotoxicity relative to baselines for both farms.

Emissions from crop residues, the spreading of manure on sur- rounding fields, and fossil fuel combustion during farm machinery operation can cause environmental hazards. Respiratory effects (or- ganics and inorganics) of air emissions are presented in Figs. S9-S10 in Supplementary Material. Non-methane volatile organic compounds (NMVOCs) and ammonia emissions are major contributors to re- spiratory organic and inorganic impacts, respectively. Similar trends were observed for these impacts on both farms except for low protein and combined feed scenarios. Respiratory organic impacts increased under low protein and combined feed scenarios while respiratory in- organic impacts decreased under these scenarios compared to the baselines on both farms. Decreased soybean meal in diets for the low protein scenario decreased respiratory inorganic impacts with reduced ammonia emissions following field application of manure. Concurrently, it affected the combined feed scenarios. On the other hand, increased corn silage in diets increased respiratory organic im- pacts for High CS and combined feed scenarios through increased NMVOC emissions. Fermentation of corn silage in storage and manure application were significant sources of NMVOC emissions. No-till es- tablishment with subsurface injection or same day incorporation of manure into the soil demonstrate the highest reduction potential, and it concurrently affects the beneficial consequences of whole-farm miti- gation plans.

58: Fig. 5 and Fig. S11 present the normalization results of environ- mental indicators associated with the large and small dairy farms, re- spectively. The small farm results are presented in the Supplementary Material because of the similarity in trends to the large farm. For both farms, terrestrial eutrophication was the highest normalized impact category across every BMP scenario; thus practices focused on reducing eutrophication in relevant farm activities may lead to larger relative improvements in the context of sustainability. The major contributing sources are primarily the decomposition of urea in manure and com- mercial fertilizer emissions of ammonia and other nitrogen compounds. Gaseous emissions of these compounds are also key drivers for re- spiratory inorganic impact. Mitigation 2 & 3 scenarios have the smallest terrestrial eutrophication potential. The no-till system with subsurface injection of manure is the most effective approach to prohibit the emissions of nitrogen compounds. Manure incorporated into the soil on the day of application and reduction in dietary protein (less purchased soybean meal) in the ration play secondary roles with a smaller impact.

63: The process-based model (IFSM) was coupled with LCA software (SimaPro ) to assess numerous dairy production scenarios based on farm-specific BMPs with the objective of identifying opportunities for

reducing environmental impacts of dairy farms in the Great Lakes re- gion of the United States. Hotspots of GHGEs were enteric methane emissions, feed production, and manure management regardless of farm size, which agrees with other studies. Use of a flare to burn the biogas produced in a covered manure storage on the small farm and a whole farm mitigation plan for the large farm demonstrate significant potential to reduce the overall carbon footprint of 20.0% and 25.8%, respectively, compared to the baselines. The evaluation of alternative rations reveals the importance of full accounting of environmental impacts by correlating not only the contribution of each feed but also diet-related effects on enteric methane emissions. While the adaptation of manure management practices showed the highest potential to mi- tigate GWP, feed production and feed-related emissions were the most
Fig. 5. Normalization results of selective impact indicators relevant to the large dairy farm BMP scenarios.

sensitive sources for other environmental impact profiles. Eutrophication impact was mainly dominated by phosphorus (P) loss in runoff following manure and commercial fertilizer application on crop fields. Reduced dietary protein achieved through more optimal use of soybean meal in diets displayed the greatest potential to alleviate aquatic eutrophication potential. The results indicate that the mitiga- tion plan in feed management practices plays an important role for enhancing sustainable dairy production systems. Although this study addressed the beneficial adaptation of dairy production practices on individual impact indicators, it is not that simple when comprehensive environmental impacts are considered as there are trade-offs among them.
